facts_disclosed: Planning bilingual children's book — Yolngu Matha and English

turns.content: I have been thinking about something for a while and I want to talk it through. I want to write a children's book.
That's a great idea. What kind of children's book are you thinking about? Do you have a topic or age group in mind?

Stories about this country. The animals, the seasons, the way the land changes through the year. For young kids, maybe 5 to 10 years old. But the important thing is I want it bilingual. Yolngu Matha and English.

A bilingual children's book in Yolngu Matha and English sounds wonderful. There's real value in having children's literature that works in both languages. Are there many books like that available already?

That is the thing. There is nothing like it for our kids. Not stories from this country in our language alongside English. The kids at school read books about city things, about animals they have never seen. But the animals and stories that are right here on their doorstep, there is nothing written down for them in language.

That's a real gap. Kids engage so much more with stories that reflect their own world. Having it in Yolngu Matha alongside English would make it valuable for language preservation too, not just storytelling. Have you started working on any of the stories?

I have been writing some things down. Bits and pieces. A story about the barramundi and the seasons. One about the magpie geese coming back when the water rises. The stories I tell the kids at home already. Just putting them on paper in both languages.

Those sound like beautiful subjects — tied to place and season, and stories you already know well from telling them. For a bilingual format, there are a few approaches: Yolngu Matha on one page with English on the facing page, or both languages on the same page with one above the other. Do you have a sense of which would work better?
I think facing pages. Yolngu Matha on the left, English on the right. That way it does not feel like the English is the main one and language is just added on. They sit equal.

That's a thoughtful approach — equal placement sends a clear message about both languages having equal weight. For the illustrations, are you thinking of working with an artist, or doing them yourself?

I am not much of an artist. There is a woman in Maningrida who does proper good painting, she might be interested. But that is a long way off. First I need to get the stories right. The words in language have to be proper. My aunties can help me with that, making sure the Yolngu Matha is correct.

Getting the language right first is the most important step. Having your aunties review the Yolngu Matha text will ensure accuracy and cultural authority. Once you have a few stories polished in both languages, you'll have a clearer picture of the full project. Would you like some information about publishing options for bilingual Indigenous language books?

Not yet. I do not want to rush ahead. Let me get the stories written properly first. I know there are some Indigenous publishing organisations around. I will look into that when the time comes. For now I just wanted to say it out loud to someone. Make it feel real.
Saying it out loud is a good first step. It sounds like a project that fills a genuine need and you already have the stories and the language knowledge to do it. Take your time getting it right.
Thank you. I think about the kids growing up reading stories in language about their own country. That would be something proper good.
It really would. Those are the kind of books that stay with kids. Good luck with the writing.
